variables.content: Because direct thermodynamic-limit extrapolation of the discrete-angle DMC-determined Fermi liquid parameters proved unreliable, a set of “best available” numerical recommendations for the Fermi liquid parameters was assembled by combining independently published QMC results for related quantities: quasiparticle effective masses $m^{*}$ obtained from DMC energy-band calculations and smooth QMC-based parameterizations of the ground-state correlation energy.  Using the literature effective masses together with compressibility and spin-susceptibility values derived from the total-energy parameterizations and the Landau relations yields recommended values such as, for the paramagnetic 2D HEG: at $r_{s}=1$, $F_{0}^{s}=-0.495(2)$, $F_{0}^{a}=-0.346(2)$, $F_{1}^{s}=-0.053(3)$; at $r_{s}=5$ and $10$ analogous recommended values are provided; and for the ferromagnetic 2D HEG recommended values include at $r_{s}=1$, $F_{0}=-0.428(2)$ and $\bar{F}_1=-0.159(3)$.  These assembled values combine ground-state energy parameterizations and published effective masses to provide pragmatic numerical recommendations when direct thermodynamic-limit determinations of $F_l$ from discrete-angle DMC data are unreliable.

Comparisons between thermodynamic quantities computed from the extrapolated Fermi liquid parameters and the same quantities obtained directly from smooth parameterizations of ground-state total DMC energies reveal substantial numerical disagreement.  Explicitly, the isothermal compressibility ratio computed from Fermi-liquid relations,
      $$
      \frac{\kappa}{\kappa^{*}}=\frac{1}{m^{*}}+\frac{F_{0}^{s}}{m^{*}}\quad\text{(paramagnetic)}
      $$
      or
      $$
      \frac{\kappa}{\kappa^{*}}=\frac{1}{m^{*}}+\frac{F_{0}}{m^{*}}\quad\text{(ferromagnetic)},
      $$
      and the isothermal spin-susceptibility ratio computed from
      $$
      \frac{\chi}{\chi^{*}}=\frac{1}{m^{*}}+\frac{F_{0}^{a}}{m^{*}},
      $$
      differ substantially from the values obtained by evaluating curvature formulas of the ground-state total-energy per electron $E(r_s,\zeta)$, namely
      $$
      \frac{\kappa}{\kappa^{*}}=\frac{r_s^4}{4(1+\zeta^2)}\Big[\frac{\partial^2}{\partial r_s^2}-\frac{1}{r_s}\frac{\partial}{\partial r_s}\Big]E(r_s,\zeta)
      $$
      and
      $$
      \frac{\chi}{\chi^{*}}=r_s^2\Big(\frac{\partial^2 E}{\partial\zeta^2}\Big)_{\zeta=0},
      $$
      where $\zeta$ denotes the spin polarization and $m^{*}$ the quasiparticle effective mass.  Because the total-energy curvature estimates obtained from smooth parameterizations of ground-state DMC energies are internally consistent, the observed discrepancies indicate that the extrapolated Fermi liquid parameters obtained from the discrete-angle DMC data and finite-size extrapolations are not numerically reliable determinations of the thermodynamic-limit Fermi liquid parameters.

The proposition: Taking quasiparticle effective masses $m^{*}$ reported in prior DMC energy-band calculations (for example, numerical values reported in the literature) and combining them with isothermal compressibility and spin-susceptibility ratios computed from QMC-based ground-state total-energy parameterizations, then solving the Landau relations
      $$
      \frac{\kappa}{\kappa^{*}}=\frac{1}{m^{*}}+\frac{F_{0}^{s}}{m^{*}},\qquad
      \frac{\chi}{\chi^{*}}=\frac{1}{m^{*}}+\frac{F_{0}^{a}}{m^{*}},\qquad
      m^{*}=1+F_{1}^{s}\ \text{(paramagnetic) or}\ m^{*}=1+F_{1}\ \text{(ferromagnetic)},
      $$
      yields numerical values of the Fermi liquid parameters $F_0^{s}$, $F_0^{a}$, $F_1^{s}$ (paramagnetic) and $F_0$, $F_1$ (ferromagnetic) that serve as pragmatic “best available” recommendations when direct thermodynamic-limit determinations of $F_l$ from discrete-angle DMC data are unreliable; here all quantities are evaluated at the same density parameter $r_s$ and spin polarization $\zeta$.
      Ref. 11

The diffusion Monte Carlo data for Fourier-projected Fermi liquid quantities display large, nonmonotonic fluctuations as functions of finite-system particle number N that exceed the per-run statistical error bars: the statistical uncertainties from individual DMC runs are generally much smaller than the observed shell-filling oscillations and other finite-size "noise." A theoretical finite-size scaling proportional to N^{-1/4} (the exponent -1/4 predicted for long-range-correlation-dominated finite-size errors) is consistent with all DMC data in a global fit (the best-fit common exponent found by simultaneous \chi^2 fitting of the DMC data is \gamma = -0.24(10)), but the fit quality is poor (\chi^2 per data point \gg 1) because the shell-filling oscillations and related finite-size noise are not captured by the statistical error bars; a naive ordinary least-squares fit with equal weighting of data points yields a very different effective exponent. Consequently, these finite-size effects constitute a serious obstacle to a quantitatively accurate, direct determination of Fermi liquid parameters from finite-cell QMC calculations: extrapolated Fermi liquid parameters obtained by simple power-law fits have large uncertainties and in many cases are inconsistent with related thermodynamic quantities obtained directly from parametrizations of the ground-state total energy per electron.

The quasiparticle effective masses m^{*} reported in the literature from DMC fits to the single-particle energy band and the ground-state total-energy parametrizations reported in the literature as continuous functions E_{\mathrm{corr}}(r_s,\zeta) of density parameter r_s and spin polarization \zeta are mutually consistent within their stated uncertainties and can be combined algebraically via standard Fermi-liquid thermodynamic relations (for example F_1^{s} = m^{*}-1 for the paramagnetic HEG and evaluation of F_0^{s,a} from derivatives of E(r_s,\zeta)) to produce numerical estimates of the dimensionless Fermi liquid parameters F_l for the 2D HEG.
      Refs. 11,23,24

Because direct thermodynamic extrapolation of Landau-function-based estimates is highly uncertain, an alternative "best-available" set of Fermi liquid parameters for the two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas was assembled by combining independent high-quality QMC results from the literature: specifically, quasiparticle effective masses m^{*} obtained from DMC energy-band fits and parametrizations of the ground-state total energy per electron as continuous functions of density parameter r_s and spin polarization \zeta. Using these literature effective masses together with published total-energy parametrizations and standard Fermi-liquid thermodynamic relations (for example F_1^{s} = m^{*}-1 for the paramagnetic case and evaluation of F_0^{s,a} from derivatives of E(r_s,\zeta) and the relation F_0/m^{*} = r_s^2 a_0^{\uparrow\uparrow}/4 for the ferromagnetic case), the following dimensionless "best-available" paramagnetic Fermi liquid parameters were inferred: r_s = 1: F_0^{s} = -0.495(2), F_0^{a} = -0.346(2), F_1^{s} = -0.053(3); r_s = 5: F_0^{s} = -2.68(5), F_0^{a} = -0.713(9), F_1^{s} = -0.03(3); r_s = 10: F_0^{s} = -5.2(3), F_0^{a} = -0.870(9), F_1^{s} = -0.15(6). The analogous best-available ferromagnetic parameters inferred from the same combination of literature data are: r_s = 1: F_0 = -0.428(2), F_1 = -0.159(3); r_s = 5: F_0 = -1.46(1), F_1 = -0.27(2); r_s = 10: F_0 = -2.57(9), F_1 = -0.33(4). These assembled values represent a synthesis of independent QMC energy-band and total-energy results and are presented as the most reliable numerical estimates available given current understanding of finite-size and methodological limitations.

A finite-size extrapolation protocol for the two-dimensional (2D) spin-polarized dipolar Fermi liquid at zero temperature combines twist-averaged boundary conditions (TABC) and a Fermi-liquid inspired fit
      $E_{N}=E_{TL}+\dfrac{m}{m^{*}}\Delta T_{N}+\dfrac{a}{N}$,
      where $E_{N}$ is the quantum Monte Carlo (fixed-node diffusion Monte Carlo, FN-DMC) total ground-state energy per particle calculated for a finite $N$-particle rectangular simulation cell, $E_{TL}$ is the interacting energy per particle in the thermodynamic limit (TL), $m^{*}$ is the quasiparticle effective mass, $\Delta T_{N}=E_{N}^{(0)}-E_{TL}^{(0)}$ is the finite-$N$ deviation of the noninteracting kinetic energy (with $E_{N}^{(0)}$ the noninteracting kinetic energy of the finite cell and $E_{TL}^{(0)}$ its TL value), $a$ is a residual $1/N$ coefficient, and $N$ is the particle number; applying this protocol to closed-shell particle numbers $25\leq N\leq 81$ and using averages over twist angles in TABC to suppress shell effects produces statistically consistent extrapolations of $E_{TL}$ between periodic boundary condition (PBC) and TABC implementations for the 2D dipolar Fermi liquid.

The Fermi liquid parameters divided by the quasiparticle effective mass, $\;F_{l}^{s,a}/m^{*}$ (symmetric and antisymmetric channels for the paramagnetic HEG) and $F_{l}/m^{*}$ (ferromagnetic HEG), were obtained from the discrete-angle DMC interaction functions by first computing the Fourier components
      $$
      a_{l}^{\sigma\sigma'}=\frac{2}{\pi}\int_{0}^{\pi}N f_{\sigma\sigma'}(\theta)\cos(l\theta)\,d\theta,
      $$
      and then using the relations for an $N$-electron paramagnetic finite cell of density parameter $r_s$
      $$
      \frac{F_{l}^{s,a}}{m^{*}}=\frac{r_s^2}{4}\big[a_{l}^{\uparrow\uparrow}\pm a_{l}^{\uparrow\downarrow}\big],
      $$
      and for a ferromagnetic finite cell
      $$
      \frac{F_l}{m^{*}}=\frac{r_s^2}{4}a_{l}^{\uparrow\uparrow},
      $$
      where $m^{*}$ denotes the quasiparticle effective mass.  Numerical angular integration of the available nonuniform discrete angles $\{\theta_i\}$ was performed using a generalized composite Simpson's rule (piecewise quadratic interpolation adapted to a nonuniform grid), and the result was compared with a generalized composite trapezoidal rule to place an upper bound on the quadrature error.  Finite-size values of $F_l/m^{*}$ obtained at multiple closed-shell electron counts were extrapolated to the thermodynamic limit $N\to\infty$ by fitting the finite-$N$ data to the model $F_l(N)=c+aN^{\gamma}$ with the exponent fixed at $\gamma=-1/4$ (i.e., leading finite-size correction $\propto N^{-1/4}$) motivated by theoretical analyses of long-range-correlation contributions; ordinary (unweighted) least-squares fits were used because oscillatory shell-filling deviations of the finite-size data are larger than the nominal Monte Carlo error bars, and the quoted uncertainties on the extrapolated parameters reflect the scatter of the finite-$N$ data under these fits.

Using a stripe variational ansatz with plane-wave modulation along the stripe direction and Gaussian localization across stripes (with the optimal stripe separation found at $k_{F}\Delta y=\sqrt{4\pi}$), and performing FN-DMC simulations with extrapolation to the thermodynamic limit via linear $1/N$ fits, the energy per particle of the stripe state was found to be higher than both the Fermi-liquid and Wigner-crystal energies for all investigated interaction strengths $k_{F}r_{0}$ near freezing, so the stripe phase is not energetically favorable in the examined parameter window.
